segments.text: Welcome to your daily dose of robotics and automation news for July 18th, 2026.
Today we cover Kimi's model breakthrough, new LeRobot AI tools, and major automotive partnerships.
Stay informed with us.
From gizmodo.com, "Kimi K3 Outperforms OpenAI and Anthropic Models".
This report details the performance of Moonshot's Kimi K3 model in comparative benchmarks against models from OpenAI and Anthropic.
Independent testing indicates that the Kimi K3 model achieved superior results on various leaderboards despite being an open-weight offering.

The Kimi K3 model incorporates 2.8 trillion parameters and is anticipated to be the largest open-weight model upon its release, challenging existing assumptions regarding the comparative pace of development between Chinese and US artificial intelligence laboratories.
This achievement has introduced heightened national security considerations concerning the ongoing competition in the AI race and the methods employed in model distillation.
The release signals a significant shift in the landscape for large language model development.
From Robotics & Automation News, Nvidia and Hugging Face expand LeRobot with new open robotics AI tools.
This report details the integration of new artificial intelligence tools into the open-source LeRobot platform.

The expansion incorporates Nvidia Isaac GR00T 1.7, an open vision-language-action model, and the Isaac Teleop framework to establish a standardized workflow for data collection, training, and deployment of artificial intelligence powered robots.
Future plans involve incorporating Nvidia Cosmos 3 to grant access to world foundation models for synthetic data generation and policy development.
This partnership intends to increase access to physical artificial intelligence technologies by merging the developer communities of both organizations.
The development indicates a push toward democratizing access to sophisticated robotics AI capabilities.
From Robotics & Automation News, Toyota and Nvidia expand partnership to develop AI for vehicles, factories and cities.
This collaboration focuses on deploying Nvidia's computing power, AI software, and simulation tools across automotive, industrial, and urban sectors.
The partnership involves utilizing Nvidia models to accelerate automotive software engineering and the creation of digital twins for factory simulation.
Furthermore, the effort includes developing multimodal vision-language models for urban traffic analysis through Woven by Toyota.
This work targets the construction of the necessary AI infrastructure for autonomous vehicles, intelligent manufacturing processes, and responsive urban environments.
The development signals a tangible progression toward integrated physical AI systems across complex operational domains.
From Robotics & Automation News, Fujitsu partners with Fanuc, Yaskawa and Kawasaki to advance physical AI using Nvidia technology.
This initiative focuses on developing a collaborative control platform that connects digital systems with robots and physical equipment across manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare sectors.

The collaboration involves integrating Nvidia technology to create a sovereign platform that incorporates artificial intelligence, robotics, simulation, and data analysis while ensuring cybersecurity and operational resilience.
This framework is designed to optimize production processes, automate material handling, and assist in medical tasks, addressing challenges related to labor shortages and aging workforces.
This partnership seeks to accelerate the adoption of physical AI and enhance Japan's standing in the robotics industry.
The development signals a focus on tangible applications for advanced AI in industrial settings moving forward.
From The Robot Report, Weave Robotics launches Isaac, its first mobile humanoid robot.
This report details the introduction of Isaac 1, a wheeled, humanoid robot designed for household chores, demonstrating advancements in consumer robotics accessibility.

The new device is priced at seven thousand nine hundred ninety-nine dollars or four hundred forty-nine dollars monthly and features a soft exterior with adjustable height ranging from three feet to five feet nine inches.

Isaac 1 incorporates autonomous navigation capabilities and allows for remote control through an application to schedule tasks, boasting an eight-hour battery life and privacy features such as camera shutters.
Weave Robotics positions this model as an accessible entry point into home robotics, following the development of its prior iteration.
This development suggests a growing trend toward integrating functional humanoid automation into domestic settings.
From Robotics & Automation News, mimic Robotics unveils full-stack platform for dexterous robot manipulation.

This report details the introduction of a system designed to advance general-purpose dexterous manipulation by collecting high-quality training data for artificial intelligence models mimicking human manipulation.
The system incorporates the mimic hand M1, a tendon-driven robotic hand, and the mimic wearable U1 exoskeleton.
The design ensures constant hand morphology across hardware, data collection, and model training to mitigate cross-embodiment gaps.
The hardware features fifteen degrees of freedom accompanied by force sensing and tactile sensors, supported by custom middleware that reduces latency.
This integrated approach establishes a foundation for general-purpose robotics development.
This development signals a step toward more realistic and generalized artificial intelligence in physical interaction systems.
From Robotics & Automation News, Robbyant releases LingBot-World 2.0 with hour-long real-time world generation.
This development introduces an open-sourced interactive world model capable of sustaining extended, real-time environment creation.
The system operates on a dual-agent architecture incorporating a Pilot Agent for direct action execution and a Director Agent responsible for introducing dynamic events.
This framework allows users to influence the simulated environment through text prompts, enabling actions such as modifying weather conditions within the virtual space.

The model maintains session stability while producing high-definition output at 720p resolution and sixty frames per second, facilitating collaborative access for multiple users within a single persistent environment.
This advancement signals significant progress in the capability of generative AI systems to manage complex, long-form interactive simulations.
From IEEE Spectrum - Robotics, "Video Friday: Your Robot Surgeon Will See You Now"
features an evaluation of humanoid technology for laparoscopic surgery, assessing technical feasibility and clinical readiness.
Research presented in this feature investigates the potential of humanoid robots in surgical procedures by evaluating the necessary technical feasibility and clinical readiness for such applications.

The study specifically evaluates advancements in locomotion and learning frameworks, including the APT-RL framework demonstrated in quadrupedal robots such as the KAIST HOUND, which facilitates high-speed, multi-skill movement.

Furthermore, new models like Lumo-2 advance the field of generalist embodied robot learning, alongside an exploration of the biomechanics inherent in flapping-wing locomotion when compared to robotic systems.
This research points toward advancing practical applications for robotic systems in both medical and general locomotion domains.
From Robotics & Automation News, Agility Robotics opens new Fremont facility to accelerate physical AI development.

This report details the opening of a sixty thousand square foot facility in Fremont, California, which serves as the company's software and capabilities hub for training and testing artificial intelligence that enhances humanoid robot performance.

The new site establishes Agility’s Bay Area Physical AI development hub within Silicon Valley and will accommodate nearly two hundred employees dedicated to developing AI capabilities for the Digit humanoid robot.

This expansion directly addresses growing enterprise demand for safe and productive humanoid robots suitable for warehouse and manufacturing environments through focused development in this new location.
This move signals an increased focus on deploying physical AI solutions in industrial settings moving forward.
From Robotics & Automation News, McLaren Construction to deploy autonomous robots at scale in partnership with FieldAI.

This story details a collaboration involving the deployment of autonomous quadruped robots on UK construction sites designed to capture imagery, generate point cloud data, and perform progress verification against design models.

The initiative utilizes FieldAI's Field Foundation Models to enable flexible operation within complex environments, allowing the robots to execute tasks such as deviation analysis and quality control checks at delivery.
This integration aims to enhance installation accuracy and reduce rework by providing precise spatial data directly from the construction environment.
The successful deployment supports McLaren Construction's ongoing digital transformation strategy in the building sector.
Future developments in field robotics are expected to drive further adoption of foundation models in industrial settings.
From The Robot Report, "With new funding, Monumental plans to bring its construction robots to the U.S."
This report details how Monumental secured thirty-two million dollars in Series B funding with the objective of expanding its construction robotics into the United States.
The company intends to utilize these funds for team growth, a market launch within the U.S., and broadening robot capabilities beyond bricklaying.

These autonomous robots employ sensors and artificial intelligence software to place bricks with millimeter accuracy on construction sites, functioning as subcontractors to augment skilled laborers by managing repetitive masonry tasks.
The company is noted for its adaptable, software-driven methodology, positioning it uniquely within the fragmented U.S. market based on European success.
This expansion signals further integration of advanced robotics into traditional construction sectors.
From The Robot Report, Founder of Maximo discusses how robotics is accelerating solar construction.

This segment details how an AI-enabled robotics startup is streamlining the installation of solar modules through automated vision systems to enhance speed and safety in renewable energy infrastructure development.
Deise Yumi Asami, founder of Maximo, explains that the company utilizes AI-based vision technology to automate the placement of solar modules using smart field deployment robotics.
This methodology employs advanced AI vision to guide robotic systems in executing complex installation tasks, thereby reshaping utility-scale solar construction processes.
Asami also noted the context of establishing the company within The AES Corp. innovation group.
This development indicates a significant trend toward integrating advanced artificial intelligence and robotics into large-scale energy infrastructure projects moving forward.
From Robotics & Automation News, Strider Robotics demonstrates a quadruped robot capable of carrying a forty kilogram payload while maintaining stability on difficult terrain.
The company is transitioning from prototyping to commercial deployment with field pilots from an oil and gas company and an automotive manufacturer.
These robots are developed as autonomous quadruped systems integrated with an artificial intelligence software platform designed for industrial inspection, navigation, and analytics.
The demonstration showcased the robot's ability to manage significant weight while traversing complex environments through sophisticated control algorithms and sensor integration.
Strider Robotics maintains a focus on increasing domestic robotics manufacturing by developing indigenous components for these advanced systems.
This development signals advancing capabilities for autonomous systems in heavy industry sectors.
Future developments in this field will be monitored closely by the industry.
From IEEE Spectrum - Robotics, "This Graduate Student Equips NASA’s Robots With Assembly Skills".
This story details a method developed for robotic assembly that enables space robots to correctly install satellite antennas in orbit.
The work involved the development of an algorithm by a graduate student in collaboration with NASA and the U.S.
Air Force.
This system allows the robots to execute the precise task of inserting antennas into their designated locations on satellites through sophisticated programming.
The methodology involves an algorithm designed to manage the complex spatial requirements for assembly operations in the vacuum environment of space.
This achievement demonstrates advanced capabilities in robotics applied to critical space technology applications.
Future developments in autonomous systems and remote operations will build upon this demonstrated capability.

From Robotics & Automation News, Luyten opens early reservations for its Ascend A27 automated construction platform, marking a significant step in the commercial deployment of advanced construction robotics.

The company has initiated early reservations for its Ascend A27 system, which transforms conventional tower cranes into robotic systems capable of automated large-scale three-dimensional concrete printing.
This platform integrates robotics, artificial intelligence, digital manufacturing, and machine control to automate building processes while leveraging existing crane infrastructure.

Early reservation customers will receive priority production allocation and an introductory launch offer, with Luyten intending for these initial customers to establish the first commercial deployment group for the new technology.
This development signals a tangible progression toward integrating sophisticated AI and automation directly into heavy industrial construction methodologies.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Jon Roberts of Inteq: ‘The software layer is where automation investment is won or lost’"

details how success in automation investment is determined by the software layer, emphasizing that Warehouse Execution Systems function as the central brain connecting hardware and personnel for real-time control.
Software-first strategies are emerging as a replacement for hardware-led approaches to achieve greater flexibility and prevent system fragmentation within automation deployments.
Autonomous mobile robots are establishing themselves as core infrastructure, supported by scalable models such as Robotics-as-a-Service which demonstrate measurable results.

Artificial intelligence drives predictive operations concerning tasks like maintenance and dynamic task allocation, necessitating robust orchestration platforms for optimal effectiveness across these systems.
Future success in the industry depends on developing a hardware-agnostic, integrated software platform allowing automation to adapt seamlessly to evolving business requirements.
From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Scott Zerkle of Panasonic Connect: ‘AI is only as good as the data it receives’"

reports on how artificial intelligence finds its value in electronics manufacturing through the analysis of machine data for predictive maintenance and defect detection rather than worker replacement.
The future of smart manufacturing involves connecting data across machines materials and processes enabling factories to learn from production history.
Manufacturers are addressing challenges with component miniaturization by implementing real-time correction during surface mount technology assembly.
Automation supports consistency across the five Ms human machine material method and measurement to maintain quality and resilience.
The most significant future impact stems from converging artificial intelligence sensing and automation into a unified interconnected manufacturing ecosystem.
This convergence signals a shift toward deeply integrated, data-driven operational systems in industrial settings.

From Robotics & Automation News, Aetina adds support for Nvidia Jetson T3000 and T2000 AI modules, expanding its portfolio to support new Nvidia AI modules for robotics and industrial edge artificial intelligence.
Aetina is integrating these modules into its forthcoming DeviceEdge AIE-KT and AIE-PT systems, which are fan-based and fanless designs, respectively.
The Jetson T3000 offers 865 FP4 TFLOPS at up to seventy watts of power consumption, while the T2000 provides four hundred FP4 TFLOPS for applications such as autonomous mobile robots.
This integration aims to deliver scalable and power-efficient AI computing options across various robotics deployments.
Nvidia anticipates that these modules will be available starting in the first quarter of 2027.
This development signals further integration of high-performance edge AI hardware into specialized industrial robotics platforms.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Smart glasses are deeply creepy. Why are celebrities like Kylie Jenner endorsing them?".
This report examines the privacy and safety concerns surrounding smart glasses and the endorsement of such technology by public figures.
The technology involves embedded facial recognition capabilities that introduce significant surveillance risks.
Concerns have been raised regarding the potential for non-consensual recording and sexual abuse, particularly for women, facilitated by these devices.
Despite safety features implemented by companies like Meta, the presence of facial recognition technology generates anxiety about pervasive monitoring and governmental oversight.
The endorsement strategy by celebrities serves to normalize these concerns about continuous personal surveillance.
Future development necessitates increased scrutiny regarding data governance within augmented reality systems.
From The Tech Buzz, "Beyond Tech: Your Weekend Upgrade"
highlights consumer technology developments, noting a rebound in electric vehicle sales and advancements in artificial intelligence across platforms.
Developments include the release of iOS 27 featuring Siri AI powered by Apple Intelligence and Google Gemini, alongside OpenAI's introduction of a new voice-first smart speaker.
Furthermore, innovations are present in the domains of art, sports, futurism, wellness, and food and drink sectors.
A strategy playbook has also been offered to assist eCommerce brands in scaling external traffic effectively through optimized channel strategies.
This playbook details methods for maximizing revenue generation while managing advertising expenditures responsibly.
The ongoing integration of advanced AI systems signals a continued acceleration in personalized interaction and automated systems development across the industry.
From technologyreview.com, "Perimenopause misinformation and treatment realities"
reports on the often inaccurate information surrounding perimenopause and the realities of available treatments.
Hormone Replacement Therapy may offer some symptom relief for some individuals but presents limitations during this phase and carries risks such as abnormal bleeding.
The report indicates that marketing for various supplements lacks robust evidence, and not all midlife symptoms are directly attributable to hormonal fluctuations.
Therefore, seeking professional medical advice is advised over relying on social media trends for symptom management.
This underscores the necessity of evidence-based information in health discussions.
The technology and information sectors should focus on validating health data streams moving forward.
From techbuzz.ai, "Debit card fights hunger funding meals through purchases".
This report details the launch of a new debit card designed to combat hunger by utilizing merchant transaction fees for relief funding.
The EAT Card operates through an application and allocates two percent of every swipe directly to hunger relief organizations without imposing any additional fees on the cardholder.
Funds are disbursed via standard ACH transfers from a US bank account, and the card is issued under Visa licensing by i3 Bank.
Furthermore, the system incorporates rewards for users, offering up to fifteen percent cash back and points.
A separate business card initiative exists through a partnership with Crowded for organizational funding purposes.
This development demonstrates the potential for consumer finance tools to integrate direct charitable mechanisms into daily transactions.
Future developments in fintech integration are anticipated.
From thehustle.co, "Fragrance clubs connect people through scent".
This report details the emergence of fragrance clubs as a method for social connection by transforming individual scent experiences into collective group activities across various global cities.
These clubs organize events such as blind-sniff meetups workshops and fragrance swaps.
The trend is supported by growth in the fragrance industry and a recognized need for in-person socialization among some populations.

These initiatives cultivate community by emphasizing personal emotional responses to scents rather than complex technical knowledge, establishing shared sensory experiences akin to book or wine clubs.
The movement highlights a demand for accessible methods of interpersonal connection.
Future developments may see integration of sensory technology into social platforms.
And that’s all the circuits we have for today!
Kimi just put on a masterclass, proving some models can really think outside the box.
Thanks for powering up your curiosity with us.
Until tomorrow, keep building!
